text:
As expected, the central theme of Sir 6:18-37 is the pursuit of wisdom that culminates in the attainment of wisdom. The section is organized in three unequal movements: the initial section deals with the fundamental condition for undergoing training in 6:18-22; the second expands on the complete subjugation to the training of wisdom in 6:23-31; the third outlines the practice that must be undertaken in order to attain wisdom in 6:32-37.

The first (initial) section of the first aera of the sixth acroatic song treats of the first members of the first movement of the second Aria, and consequently of the first Aria of the song, that of the first aera. The first movement (Sir 6:18-22) is made up of two members, quite opposed to each other. The first (Sir 6:18-19) alludes to the labors of husbandry, and with this view exhorts the disciple to perseverance. The scribe shows that the progress of wisdom is long and laborious; but in the same proportion that there is labor, there will be afterwards fruit and harvest in abundance, if the disciple but takes to this labor.

Secondly, the second section of our text describes the tragic state of those who do not enter this path of education. The author says the uneducated are often called ‘heartless’; that is, people of no intellectual or moral fibres. They were not patient enough to travel the long road and could not carry the ‘stones of trial’ on the shorter routes, so they did not persevere at all. In our text the whole passage (with the possible exception of the first verse) focuses on the possibility of acquiring wisdom in this world. Apparently the question is not whether wisdom can be acquired, as it is clearly possible (v. 20: (as we have seen) everybody from everywhere (who has the right preparation) has access to the means of education. The question is how few enter upon this road to wisdom. According to our author access to wisdom is not a matter of social class or of birth (who is in a position to influence it?); it is simply a question of individual dispositions, such as one’s power to endure hardship and one’s degree of patience for gradual progress. So only the few have the needed dispositions and therefore become wise.
explanation: Contains unusual terminology (acroatic/aera) and a slightly stilted, highly structured scholarly tone that AI often produces; however, the content could also come from a human academic.
